1. 1 Introduction
  2. 2 Cosmologically coupled black holes
  3. 3 Delay time and the mass correcting factor
  4. 4 Direct constraints from the observed events
  5. 5 Constraints using the power-law-plus-peak distribution
    1. 5.1 General procedures
    2. 5.2 Results
  6. 6 Modified delay-time distribution from CCBH physics
  7. 7 Conclusions
  8. A CCBH and minimum mass
  9. B CCBH-modified delay-time distributions compared with the log-uniform one: general approach
  10. C Changing the delay time distribution slope

arXiv:2307.02474v2 [astro-ph.CO] 13 Jan 2024

Constraints on cosmologically coupled black holes from gravitational wave observations and minimal formation mass

Luca Amendola11{}^{1}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 1 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT, Davi C. Rodrigues1,2,3123{}^{1,2,3}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 1 , 2 , 3 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT, Sumit Kumar4,545{}^{4,5}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 4 , 5 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT, Miguel Quartin3,6,7367{}^{3,6,7}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 3 , 6 , 7 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT
11{}^{1}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 1 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT Institut für Theoretische Physik, Universität Heidelberg, Philosophenweg 16, 69120. Heidelberg, Germany
22{}^{2}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT Departamento de Física & Cosmo-Ufes, Universidade Federal do Espírito Santo, 29075-910. Vitória - ES, Brazil
33{}^{3}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 3 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT PPGCosmo, Universidade Federal do Espírito Santo, 29075-910, Vitória - ES, Brazil
44{}^{4}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 4 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT Max-Planck-Institut für Gravitationsphysik (Albert-Einstein-Institut), D-30167 Hannover, Germany
55{}^{5}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 5 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT Leibniz Universität Hannover, D-30167 Hannover, Germany
66{}^{6}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 6 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT Instituto de Física, Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro, 21941-972, Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil
77{}^{7}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT 7 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT Observatório do Valongo, Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro, 20080-090, Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brazil
(January 13, 2024)
Abstract

We test the possibility that the black holes (BHs) detected by LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) may be cosmologically coupled and grow in mass proportionally to the cosmological scale factor to some power k𝑘kitalic_k, which may also act as the dark energy source if k≈3𝑘3k\approx 3italic_k ≈ 3. This approach was proposed as an extension of Kerr BHs embedded in cosmological backgrounds and possibly without singularities or horizons. In our analysis, we develop and apply two methods to test these cosmologically coupled BHs (CCBHs) either with or without connection to dark energy. We consider different scenarios for the time between the binary BH formation and its merger, and we find that the standard log-uniform distribution yields weaker constraints than the CCBH-corrected case. Assuming that the minimum mass of a BH with stellar progenitor is 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, we estimate the probability that at least one BH among the observed ones had an initial mass below this threshold. We obtain these probabilities either directly from the observed data or by assuming the LVK power-law-plus-peak mass distribution. In the latter case we find, at 2⁢σ2𝜎2\sigma2 italic_σ level, that k<2.1𝑘2.1k<2.1italic_k < 2.1 for the standard log-uniform distribution, or k<1.1𝑘1.1k<1.1italic_k < 1.1 for the CCBH-corrected distribution. Slightly weaker bounds are obtained in the direct method. Considering the uncertainties on the nature of CCBHs, we also find that the required minimum CCBH mass value to eliminate the tensions for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3 should be lower than 0.5 M⊙subscript𝑀direct-productM_{\odot}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (again at 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ). Finally, we show that future observations have the potential to decisively confirm these bounds.

keywords:
black hole physics – gravitational waves – dark energy
††pubyear: 2023††pagerange: Constraints on cosmologically coupled black holes from gravitational wave observations and minimal formation mass–C

1 Introduction

Recently, a new intriguing hypothesis about the origin of the cosmic acceleration has been put forward by Croker & Weiner (2019); Croker et al. (2020b), with further developments by Farrah et al. (2023b). According to this scenario, black holes (BHs) grow in mass due to a form of cosmological coupling unrelated to local accretion. If this growth is fast enough, it could compensate the decrease in number density due to the cosmic expansion, and generate a form of effective cosmological constant. These BHs deviate from the standard Kerr solution.111Since astrophysical BHs are expected to have angular momentum, Kerr is a better description than Schwarzschild. Kerr BHs are asymptotically Minkowski and have a singularity “dressed” by a horizon. Kerr-de Sitter solutions are also known (for a review see Akcay & Matzner, 2011). There is expectation that these solutions can be found within general relativity (GR) and that they could be singularity free (see also Dymnikova & Galaktionov, 2016). These non-standard BH solutions are asymptotically Friedmann-Roberton-Walker, rather than Minkowski (Faraoni & Jacques, 2007; Croker et al., 2020a; Croker & Weiner, 2019; Croker et al., 2020b). They could provide an average pressure that would constitute the entire amount of dark energy needed to explain the cosmic acceleration if the BHs have the necessary abundance. Farrah et al. (2023b) argue that this may be the case. In a companion paper, Farrah et al. (2023a) have found strong indication in favor of just such a cosmological growth of supermassive BHs in elliptical galaxies. This growth seems very difficult to explain in terms of the standard local growth channels of accretion. From different considerations, Gao & Li (2023); Cadoni et al. (2023a, b) provide further support for cosmologically coupled black holes.

This new BH solution is at the moment a conjecture, and in fact, criticisms on the above framework within GR have appeared. For instance, Avelino (2023) considers the use of gravastars as CCBHs, criticizes the mechanism for generating cosmological pressure assuming that the Birkhoff theorem can be applied and, additionally, points out that, if CCBH momentum is conserved, SMBHs could not be at rest with respect to their host galaxy. Parnovsky (2023) criticizes both the uncertainty in the estimation of SMBH data and the possibility of BHs to provide a negative pressure. Mistele (2023) points out an inconsistency between the averaging process proposed by Croker & Weiner (2019) and the action principle, thus this work also puts into question the proposed mechanism that generates the cosmological pressure. Wang & Wang (2023) argue that a cosmological coupling cannot exist within general relativity inside gravitationally bound systems. Here we take an agnostic view and show how this may be at tension with GW data, independently of the detailed microphysics that may lead to the CCBH realization.

This paper is devoted to testing the cosmologically coupled BHs (CCBH) by looking at the current and future datasets from LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK). The current understanding is that the gravitational waves (GW) detected by LVK come from the merging of BHs with stellar progenitors. If the CCBH hypothesis is correct, they must have grown to the observed mass from an initially lower mass. However, BHs with a stellar progenitor cannot be formed with arbitrarily low masses. Observationally, there is evidence of a paucity of BH masses between 2−5⁢M⊙25subscript𝑀direct-product2-5M_{\odot}2 - 5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Özel et al., 2010; Abbott et al., 2023; de Sá et al., 2022), while there is no conclusive evidence for a BH with mass about or below 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (see also Abbott et al., 2022). Our main results are based on the conservative mass threshold mth=2.0⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th2.0subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}=2.0M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 2.0 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT as the minimum BH formation mass. However, we also consider changes in different values for this threshold. The higher (lower) is this threshold, the stronger (weaker) are our constraints. If in the future a stellar BH is detected with mass below 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT this will clearly imply that mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT has to be smaller than what is detected, weaking our constraints. In particular, the constraints would vanish for very low thresholds of ≲0.5⁢M⊙less-than-or-similar-toabsent0.5subscript𝑀direct-product\lesssim 0.5M_{\odot}≲ 0.5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. In Appendix A we present further discussions on this.

In this paper, we use two complementary approaches, explore ways to alleviate the tensions222We use the word “tension” for rejections at a level higher than 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ. we find, and briefly discuss future prospects. We conclude that the CCBH as proposed by Farrah et al. (2023b) is in strong tension with what we know about stellar progenitor BHs, but there still is an open parameter space where it can survive the present test. In particular, we find no relevant tension for the CCBH case studied by Croker et al. (2021). The forthcoming new GW datasets will soon shed further light on the CCBH conjecture.

The codes we used for this work are available at https://github.com/itpamendola/CCBH-direct and https://github.com/davi-rodrigues/CCBH-Numerics.

2 Cosmologically coupled black holes

Farrah et al. (2023a) considered three samples of red-sequence elliptical galaxies at different redshifts and found that the growth of supermassive BHs is significantly larger than the growth of stellar mass, being a factor of 20 from z∼2similar-to𝑧2z\sim 2italic_z ∼ 2 to z∼0similar-to𝑧0z\sim 0italic_z ∼ 0. This growth is too large to be compatible with the expected accretion rate (Farrah et al., 2023a). This suggests a different growth mechanism such that mBH∝m*⁢(1+z)−3.5±1.4proportional-tosubscript𝑚BHsubscript𝑚superscript1𝑧plus-or-minus3.51.4m_{\rm BH}\propto m_{*}(1+z)^{-3.5\pm 1.4}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BH end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∝ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT * end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( 1 + italic_z ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 3.5 ± 1.4 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, at 90%percent9090\%90 % confidence level, where m*subscript𝑚m_{*}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT * end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the stellar mass of the galaxy and mBHsubscript𝑚BHm_{\rm BH}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_BH end_POSTSUBSCRIPT the supermassive BH mass of the same galaxy.

A possible explanation for the above physics comes from the proposal of cosmologically coupled BHs (CCBHs) (Faraoni & Jacques, 2007; Croker & Weiner, 2019; Croker et al., 2021). In this case, BHs would grow following the parametrization (Croker et al., 2021)

m⁢(a)=m⁢(ai)⁢(aai)k,𝑚𝑎𝑚subscript𝑎𝑖superscript𝑎subscript𝑎𝑖𝑘m(a)=m(a_{i})\left(\frac{a}{a_{i}}\right)^{k}\,,italic_m ( italic_a ) = italic_m ( italic_a start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ( divide start_ARG italic_a end_ARG start_ARG italic_a start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , (1)

where k≥0𝑘0k\geq 0italic_k ≥ 0 is a constant, aisubscript𝑎𝑖a_{i}italic_a start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the cosmological scale factor at the time of the BH formation and m⁢(ai)𝑚subscript𝑎𝑖m(a_{i})italic_m ( italic_a start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is its mass at that time.

Farrah et al. (2023b) explore the viability of k≈3𝑘3k\approx 3italic_k ≈ 3, which would both explain the supermassive BHs growth and provide a source of dark energy capable of generating the observed ΩΛsubscriptΩΛ\Omega_{\Lambda}roman_Ω start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_Λ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT value. The latter requires further assumptions, in particular a proper star formation rate, that all the remnants with mass >2.7absent2.7>2.7> 2.7M⊙subscript𝑀direct-productM_{\odot}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are BHs, and that all the BHs follow the above mass parametrization. Beside the theoretical issues commented in Sec. 1, Lei et al. (2023) used JWST data and found a conflict with Farrah et al. (2023a) parametrization at redshifts z∼4.5−7similar-to𝑧4.57z\sim 4.5-7italic_z ∼ 4.5 - 7. These high redshift results are mostly independent of our analysis: the constraints we find come from lower redshifts, as will be shown when constraining the maximum redshift of binary BH formation (zmaxsubscript𝑧maxz_{\rm max}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT).

If all BHs are cosmologically coupled with k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3, Rodriguez (2023) pointed out that this would be in contradiction with globular cluster NGC 3201 data, since it would imply that one of the BHs would have a mass below 2.2⁢M⊙2.2subscript𝑀direct-product2.2M_{\odot}2.2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. A similar test on two Gaia DR3 stellar-BH systems with reliable age estimation has been carried out by Andrae & El-Badry (2023), resulting in the 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ upper limit k≤3.2𝑘3.2k\leq 3.2italic_k ≤ 3.2 assuming the same 2.2⁢M⊙2.2subscript𝑀direct-product2.2M_{\odot}2.2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT limit and fixing the background to ΛΛ\Lambdaroman_ΛCDM. In Appendix A we detail further aspects of the BH minimum mass in the context of CCBH.

Ghodla et al. (2023) found that the rate of mergers and their typical masses in a CCBH scenario would be hardly compatible with LVK observations; they also point out that CCBHs should exhibit lower spins due to their increase in mass. They have also derived a modified delay time for CCBHs that we consider in Sec. 6. This modified delay time makes CCBHs more incompatible with current data, as we develop here.

Our purpose here is to test if the BHs detected from their coalescence waves could be cosmologically coupled. Before the results from Farrah et al. (2023b), Croker et al. (2021) (see also Croker et al., 2020a) developed simulations of merging BHs and considered the impact of the cosmological coupling on the LVK detected BHs, showing that k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5 would be preferred over the standard k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0, at least for certain isolated-binary-evolution model. We use here the most recent data from LVK, together with more recent delay time expectations. A crucial difference between this work and the works of Croker et al. (2021); Ghodla et al. (2023) on CCBH and LVK data is that they started from a given BBH formation mass, assumed to be realistic, and consider if they could mimic LVK data from that initial mass distribution. Here we aim to estimate what is the probability that at least one of the observed BHs via LVK would be formed with a mass below a given threshold mass (thus in part similar to Rodriguez, 2023). A key quantity for modelling the CCBH effects on LVK data is the delay time tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (i.e., the interval between BBH formation and merger), which is detailed in Sec. 3.

Within the general class of CCBHs, we distinguish two cases. If BHs have dark energy implications and constitute its only source, as proposed in (Croker et al., 2020b; Farrah et al., 2023b), then the constant k𝑘kitalic_k, which parametrizes the energy density of BHs as a function of a⁢(t)𝑎𝑡a(t)italic_a ( italic_t ), has a direct connection with the dark energy equation of state parameter w𝑤witalic_w, with

k≡−3⁢w.𝑘3𝑤k\equiv-3w\,.italic_k ≡ - 3 italic_w . (2)

We call this scenario dark energy BHs, DEBH.

If CCBHs masses increase following Eq. (1), but if they do not constitute a dark energy source, we call these growing BHs (GBHs). For instance, Croker et al. (2021) considered BHs with this property with k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5. This picture can be realised if the CCBHs contribution to the total cosmological energy density is negligible, or if GBHs are receiving energy with another field, say an additional scalar field. In the GBH scenario, dark energy is fully sourced by a cosmological constant and there is no deviation from ΛΛ\Lambdaroman_ΛCDM background cosmology.

The two models, DEBH and GBH, have identical background cosmological evolution for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3 but diverge otherwise. We consider both in the following.

3 Delay time and the mass correcting factor

The merging of binary black holes (BBH) systems detected by LVK is commonly considered to be the end of a pair of BHs that orbited together from several Myrs to several giga-years before the merger (Abbott et al., 2021c). These BHs masses are consistent with them being remnants of star progenitors, and this constitutes the standard interpretation (e.g., Belczynski et al., 2016; Mapelli, 2020; van Son et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2022; Abbott et al., 2023).

The relevant time during which the CCBH effect (1) is active extends between the BHs formation and their merger. We call this time the BBH delay time and denote it by tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. We note that another delay-time definition, as the time between the stellar pair formation and the BBH merger, is also used in the literature. However, they typically differ by a few Myr (van Son et al., 2022), hence both definitions are essentially the same.

For the GBH scenario, we consider any value of k𝑘kitalic_k in the range 0≤k≤30𝑘30\leq k\leq 30 ≤ italic_k ≤ 3 (Croker et al., 2021), where k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0 corresponds to the standard case (uncoupled Kerr BHs). Apart from the k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3 case, other values of particular interest that have been discussed in the literature are k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5 (Croker et al., 2021) and k=1𝑘1k=1italic_k = 1 (Cadoni et al., 2023b).

For the DEBH case, changing the k𝑘kitalic_k value changes cosmology. For clarity, this case will be parameterized with a constant w𝑤witalic_w, instead of k𝑘kitalic_k. We mainly consider −0.6≤w≤−10.6𝑤1-0.6\leq w\leq-1- 0.6 ≤ italic_w ≤ - 1. We do not consider more negative w𝑤witalic_w values since they can only strengthen our constraints. The DEBH scenario has no limit that leads simultaneously to standard BHs and standard background cosmology.

Abbott et al. (2021c, 2023) state that the distribution of delay times can be approximated by a log-uniform distribution (i.e., p⁢(td)∝1/tdproportional-to𝑝subscript𝑡𝑑1subscript𝑡𝑑p(t_{d})\propto 1/t_{d}italic_p ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ∝ 1 / italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) with 0.05<td⁢(Gyrs)<13.50.05subscript𝑡𝑑Gyrs13.50.05<t_{d}(\mbox{Gyrs})<13.50.05 < italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( Gyrs ) < 13.5 for BBHs. It is also pointed out that the formation of the first BBHs is restricted to z<10𝑧10z<10italic_z < 10. This picture is in good agreement with simulations and observational constraints (e.g., Belczynski et al., 2016; van Son et al., 2022; Fishbach & Kalogera, 2021).

CCBHs, on the other hand, grow with time and therefore their delay times will also change: since larger BH masses dissipate energy faster through GW emission, the delay time of CCBHs should be smaller than for ordinary BHs for the same initial mass and orbit (Croker et al., 2020b; Farrah et al., 2023b; Ghodla et al., 2023). This does not imply that the delay time distribution of the detected BBH mergers will favour shorter times. In particular, as commented by Farrah et al. (2023b); Ghodla et al. (2023), BBHs that would not merge before z=0𝑧0z=0italic_z = 0 in the standard picture may merge if CCBH is true. We will explore in more detail the possible CCBHs changes to the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution in Sec. 6 and Appendix B. Of particular relevance, there it is shown that the CCBH corrections to the delay-time distribution favor larger delay times than the log-uniform distribution. Therefore, studying the log-uniform case is useful also because it provides conservative bounds.

Due to such unknowns, we use a log-uniform distribution for tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT varying three parameters that have a direct impact on the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values (tmin,tmaxsubscript𝑡minsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm min},\,t_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and zmaxsubscript𝑧maxz_{\rm max}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, as detailed below). Moreover, in Appendix C we explore the possibility of a steeper PDF for the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution (i.e., smaller delay times on average), which reduces the tensions we find in the main analysis.

We adopt then here the log-uniform tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution,

log⁡td∼U⁢(log⁡tmin,log⁡tx),similar-tosubscript𝑡d𝑈subscript𝑡minsubscript𝑡x\log t_{\rm d}\sim U(\log t_{\rm min},\log t_{\rm x}),roman_log italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∼ italic_U ( roman_log italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , roman_log italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) , (3)

where txsubscript𝑡xt_{\rm x}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_x end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the minimum between the maximum delay time tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and the time difference between the merger redshift (zmsubscript𝑧mz_{\rm m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) and the maximum redshift with BBH formation (zmaxsubscript𝑧maxz_{\rm max}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT). As reference values, we consider

tmin=0.05⁢Gyr,subscript𝑡min0.05Gyr\displaystyle t_{\rm min}=0.05\,{\rm Gyr},italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.05 roman_Gyr ,
tmax=13.0⁢Gyr,subscript𝑡max13.0Gyr\displaystyle t_{\rm max}=13.0\,{\rm Gyr},italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 13.0 roman_Gyr ,
zmax=10,subscript𝑧max10\displaystyle z_{\rm max}=10,italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 10 , (4)
w=−1,𝑤1\displaystyle w=-1,italic_w = - 1 ,
k=3.𝑘3\displaystyle k=3\,.italic_k = 3 .

Besides these reference values, we also explore other combinations. We anticipate that the CCBH tension that we find here either increases or stays constant if tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, zmaxsubscript𝑧maxz_{\rm max}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, or tminsubscript𝑡mint_{\rm min}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are increased. For the cosmological model we assume Ωm=0.32subscriptΩm0.32\Omega_{\rm m}=0.32roman_Ω start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.32 and H0=70subscript𝐻070H_{0}=70italic_H start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 70 km s−11{}^{-1}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT - 1 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT Mpc−11{}^{-1}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT - 1 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT.

Fishbach & Kalogera (2021) studied a possible correlation between tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT considering observational data. It was found a marginal preference for smaller masses values to have larger tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. We do not consider such a correlation here, but if future analyses confirm this mass delay-time correlation, it will result in stronger bounds on the CCBH model from GW data. In any case, the true delay-time distribution and its dependence on the mass are still uncertain (e.g., van Son et al., 2022).

Let us now consider a BH that is formed at zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and merges after a delay time tdsubscript𝑡𝑑t_{d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT at zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Then

td=∫zizmd⁢z(1+z)⁢H⁢(z).subscript𝑡𝑑superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑧𝑖subscript𝑧𝑚d𝑧1𝑧𝐻𝑧t_{d}=\int_{z_{i}}^{z_{m}}\frac{\textrm{d}z}{(1+z)H(z)}\,.italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT divide start_ARG d italic_z end_ARG start_ARG ( 1 + italic_z ) italic_H ( italic_z ) end_ARG . (5)

This can be inverted for a given w𝑤witalic_w using

H2=H02⁢[Ωm⁢(1+z)3+(1−Ωm)⁢(1+z)3+3⁢w],superscript𝐻2superscriptsubscript𝐻02delimited-[]subscriptΩ𝑚superscript1𝑧31subscriptΩ𝑚superscript1𝑧33𝑤H^{2}=H_{0}^{2}\big{[}\Omega_{m}(1+z)^{3}+(1-\Omega_{m})(1+z)^{3+3w}\big{]}\,,italic_H start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = italic_H start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT [ roman_Ω start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( 1 + italic_z ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + ( 1 - roman_Ω start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ( 1 + italic_z ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 + 3 italic_w end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ] , (6)

leading to

zi=zi⁢(zm,td).subscript𝑧𝑖subscript𝑧𝑖subscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑡𝑑z_{i}=z_{i}(z_{m},t_{d})\,.italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) . (7)

Then the initial mass misubscript𝑚𝑖m_{i}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT of BHs will be a function of zm,td,ksubscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑡𝑑𝑘z_{m},\,t_{d},\,kitalic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_k, and proportional to the mass mmsubscript𝑚𝑚m_{m}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT at merging time,

mi=mm⁢(1+zm1+zi⁢(zm,td))k.subscript𝑚𝑖subscript𝑚𝑚superscript1subscript𝑧𝑚1subscript𝑧𝑖subscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑡𝑑𝑘m_{i}=m_{m}\left(\frac{1+z_{m}}{1+z_{i}(z_{m},t_{d})}\right)^{k}.italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( divide start_ARG 1 + italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG 1 + italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) end_ARG ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT . (8)

From a given set of observed BHs masses and a tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution (3), we aim to find the probability that none of the observed BHs was formed with mass below the mass threshold mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (i.e., Mi<mthsubscript𝑀𝑖subscript𝑚thM_{i}<m_{\rm th}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT). If this probability is close to 1, then there is no tension between observations and the CCBH model. Otherwise, there is tension between the observational data, the model and the given assumptions. We will show that the latter is the case. Alternatively, one should invoke significant changes in some of the basic assumptions, e.g. a lower threshold for BH formation, shorter delay times, a late BH formation, or a different growth scheme.

We estimate this probability in two complementary ways. In the first one, we use directly the current dataset and find the joint probability that at least one BH was born with a mass below threshold under the CCBH hypothesis. Since we do not correct the observed distribution for the selection effects, we implicitly discard low-mass BHs from the estimation, and therefore we end up with more conservative, but possibly more robust, estimates. We call this the direct method. In the second method, we derive the expected initial mass distribution of BHs taking into account the selection bias of the detectors through the power-law-plus-peak (PLPP) profile (Abbott et al., 2021d, 2023). This method leads to stronger constraints. We denote this as the PLPP method.

The GWTC-3 data we use are shown in Table 1 and in Fig. 1. These data come from confident BBH and NSBH events (Abbott et al., 2021a, 2023) that satisfy pastro>0.5subscript𝑝astro0.5p_{\rm astro}>0.5italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_astro end_POSTSUBSCRIPT > 0.5 and FARminmin{}_{\rm min}start_FLOATSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_FLOATSUBSCRIPT < 1 yr−11{}^{-1}start_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT - 1 end_FLOATSUPERSCRIPT. In our analysis, we use separately either the primary m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses or the secondary m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ones. Therefore, each selected mass corresponds to an independent history of a compact binary evolution and merger. Considering all the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT in a single analysis would be incorrect since binary BHs have the same tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and would therefore not be independent. Although we consider here results with either primary or secondary masses, emphasis is given on the results for m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses, since these produce more robust and more conservative constraints. For the direct method, this choice automatically removes BHs that are outliers with particularly low mass and have a large impact on the statistics used. For the PLPP method, the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT data is more robust since its distribution depends on one less parameter, with non-negligible uncertainty, than the m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution. In principle, one could consider m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses for all the BBH cases, and change to m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses for the NSBH systems, but the impact on the results is small since there are only one or two NSBH systems in our selected sample.

The selected sample, Table 1, has only two systems classified as NSBH by LVK, namely: GW200105_162426 and GW190917_114630. This classification depends on the adopted minimum mass for BHs, and Abbott et al. (2023) consider 2.5⁢M⊙2.5subscript𝑀direct-product2.5M_{\odot}2.5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. When considering that the minimum mass is 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, there remains a single NSBH, GW200115_042309. Excluding all events with secondary mass less than 5⁢M⊙5subscript𝑀direct-product5M_{\odot}5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT as potential NS or outliers, we are left with 69 m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT data points.

4 Direct constraints from the observed events

Here we discuss the direct method. The formation redshift that a BH of merging mass mmsubscript𝑚𝑚m_{m}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT observed at zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT should have to initially form with a given threshold mass mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is given by Eq. (8) as

zth=(1+zm)⁢(mmmth)1/k−1,subscript𝑧th1subscript𝑧𝑚superscriptsubscript𝑚msubscript𝑚th1𝑘1z_{\rm th}=(1+z_{m})\left(\frac{m_{\rm m}}{m_{\rm th}}\right)^{1/k}-1\,,italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = ( 1 + italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ( divide start_ARG italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 1 / italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 1 , (9)

and the corresponding delay time is

tth=td⁢(zm,zth)=td⁢(zm,mm,mth).subscript𝑡thsubscript𝑡𝑑subscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑧thsubscript𝑡𝑑subscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑚𝑚subscript𝑚tht_{\rm th}=t_{d}(z_{m},z_{\rm th})=t_{d}(z_{m},m_{m},m_{\rm th})\,.italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) . (10)

If the delay time is larger than tthsubscript𝑡tht_{\rm th}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, the BH would have formed with a mass below the threshold. If tthsubscript𝑡tht_{\rm th}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT plus the merger age t⁢(zm)𝑡subscript𝑧𝑚t(z_{m})italic_t ( italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is larger than the cut-off tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, we take tth=tmax−t⁢(zm)subscript𝑡thsubscript𝑡max𝑡subscript𝑧𝑚t_{\rm th}=t_{{\rm max}}-t(z_{m})italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic_t ( italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ). Analogously, if zthsubscript𝑧thz_{\rm th}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is larger than, say, zmax=10subscript𝑧max10z_{\rm max}=10italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 10, we should cut it at zmaxsubscript𝑧maxz_{\rm max}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT to prevent formation at an unrealistically early epoch. Now, given a normalized delay-time distribution Ψ⁢(td)Ψsubscript𝑡𝑑\Psi(t_{d})roman_Ψ ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ), the probability that a BH has formation mass above mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is

pi⁢(zm,mm,mth)=∫0tthΨ⁢(td)⁢d⁢td.subscript𝑝𝑖subscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑚𝑚subscript𝑚thsuperscriptsubscript0subscript𝑡thΨsubscript𝑡𝑑dsubscript𝑡𝑑p_{i}(z_{m},m_{m},m_{\rm th})=\int_{0}^{t_{\rm th}}\Psi(t_{d})\textrm{d}t_{d}\,.italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_Ψ ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) d italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT . (11)

The combined probability of having N𝑁Nitalic_N BHs within the acceptable formation mass range >mthabsentsubscript𝑚th>m_{\rm th}> italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is

P⁢(m1>mth)=∏iNpi𝑃subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚thsuperscriptsubscriptproduct𝑖𝑁subscript𝑝𝑖P(m_{1}>m_{\rm th})=\prod_{i}^{N}p_{i}italic_P ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT > italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = ∏ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (12)

and therefore the probability of at least one below-threshold BH is 1−P1𝑃1-P1 - italic_P. In order to reject the CCBH hypothesis at given k𝑘kitalic_k, we should find a small P𝑃Pitalic_P for the currently observed BHs. In other words, the p𝑝pitalic_p-value for rejecting the CCBH hypothesis is p=P⁢(m1>mth)𝑝𝑃subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚thp=P(m_{1}>m_{\rm th})italic_p = italic_P ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT > italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ).

Since GW observations pick preferentially high-mass BHs, the constraints we derive are on the conservative side.

We consider both the DEBH case, in which the BH growth is linked to the dark energy so that k=−3⁢w𝑘3𝑤k=-3witalic_k = - 3 italic_w, and the alternative GBH scenario in which the BH growth does not influence the cosmological expansion. For simplicity, in this second case, we fix w=−1𝑤1w=-1italic_w = - 1, i.e. the standard cosmological constant. In each case, there is then just one BH-cosmological parameter (in addition to the astrophysical ones, namely tmin,tmax,zmax,mthsubscript𝑡minsubscript𝑡maxsubscript𝑧maxsubscript𝑚tht_{\rm min},\,t_{\rm max},\,z_{\rm max},\,m_{\rm th}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT): either k𝑘kitalic_k for the GBH case, or w𝑤witalic_w for the DEBH case.

We also notice that the merger redshifts zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are obtained from the luminosity distance by assuming a ΛΛ\Lambdaroman_ΛCDM evolution. However, in the DEBH scenario, the background is ΛΛ\Lambdaroman_ΛCDM only for w=−1𝑤1w=-1italic_w = - 1 so for any other value of w𝑤witalic_w we should derive a new set of zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. This correction is however on average Δ⁢z=0.02Δ𝑧0.02\Delta z=0.02roman_Δ italic_z = 0.02 for w=−0.6𝑤0.6w=-0.6italic_w = - 0.6, and smaller for w𝑤witalic_w closer to −11-1- 1. This is negligible with respect to the current uncertainty in zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, so we neglect it.

We illustrate in the corner plot Fig. 2 the exclusion plots for various combinations of parameters for both scenarios, implicitly fixing all the other parameters to the reference case described above in Eq. (3). In this and in the subsequent corner plot, the reference case (for which GBH and DEBH coincide) is always at the upper right corner; moving beyond this point increases the rejection level of the CCBH hypothesis.

The main result is that, for DEBH and in the reference case, the probability of having no BHs below threshold is 0.0083, corresponding to 2.64σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ. Using instead the m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses, and excluding as potential outliers (perhaps neutron stars) the two compact objects with masses in the range 2−5⁢M⊙25subscript𝑀direct-product2-5M_{\odot}2 - 5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, we obtain, as expected, a higher rejection level of 3.05σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ. Decreasing w𝑤witalic_w into the phantom regime w<−1𝑤1w<-1italic_w < - 1 makes the result stronger. For w<−1.2𝑤1.2w<-1.2italic_w < - 1.2, using m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses the rejection is at the 3σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ level (again, fixing all the other parameters to reference). For the other parameters, the range for which the tension is reduced below the 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ level are tmax<8.7subscript𝑡max8.7t_{\rm max}<8.7italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 8.7 Gyr and zmax<4subscript𝑧max4z_{\rm max}<4italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 4.

For k≈3𝑘3k\approx 3italic_k ≈ 3, the dependence on the threshold mass is shown in Fig. 3. Using the references values, the tension is removed only if the minimum BH mass is lower than 1.1⁢M⊙1.1subscript𝑀direct-product1.1M_{\odot}1.1 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3. Within the DEBH scenario, that is, considering changes of the dark energy equation of state induced by k=−3⁢w𝑘3𝑤k=-3witalic_k = - 3 italic_w, the minimum BH mass should be below 1.4M⊙subscript𝑀direct-productM_{\odot}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for w=−0.9𝑤0.9w=-0.9italic_w = - 0.9, or below 0.9M⊙subscript𝑀direct-productM_{\odot}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for w=−1.1𝑤1.1w=-1.1italic_w = - 1.1.

In Fig. 4 we show the distribution of probabilities for 1000 realizations of the current data randomly chosen. The mass and redshift distribution for each event has been obtained from the posteriors samples of the latest GWTC data release (Abbott et al., 2019, 2021b, 2021a). We use source frame mass distribution for each event. This narrow distribution shows that sticking with the best fit zm,mmsubscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑚𝑚z_{m},\,m_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values is an acceptable approximation. For the forecast and k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3, the rejection level goes beyond 5σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ. On the other hand, for k=1𝑘1k=1italic_k = 1 even the forecasted data implies no relevant tension.

In the same Fig. 4 we also estimate P⁢(m1>mth)𝑃subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚thP(m_{1}>m_{\rm th})italic_P ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT > italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) for a number of future events. After 4 months LVK O4 run has observed 44 significant BBH candidate events.333https://gracedb.ligo.org/superevents/public/O4/ If we further limit ourselves to those with preliminary FAR<min1/yr{}_{\rm min}<1/{\rm yr}start_FLOATSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_FLOATSUBSCRIPT < 1 / roman_yr and preliminary BBH classification with over 90% probability, 35 BBH candidates remain. Considering that O4 should last for 20 months, and that Virgo has not yet joined observations, one expects as a very conservative lower bound a total of 20/4 ×\times× 35 = 175 new BBH events (or 247 in total) by the end of O4. Therefore we assumed 250 BBH events for our O4 forecasts in Fig. 4 and below.

Fig. 5 explores the two most relevant parameters for this analysis, k𝑘kitalic_k and minimum BH mass (mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT), considering the observational distribution of the pair (zm,m1)subscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑚1(z_{m},m_{1})( italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) for each detected event. We use here the GBH picture, since the DEBH one changes significantly the cosmology if k𝑘kitalic_k is not close to 3. The main result is the red solid line that delimits the 2⁢σ2𝜎2\sigma2 italic_σ excluded region using the current LVK data. To find this curve we proceed as follows: for each pair of values (k,mth)𝑘subscript𝑚th(k,m_{\rm th})( italic_k , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) and a particular realization of the observational data distribution, we compute the probability that there is no BH with mass below mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. We repeat the previous evaluation for 300 realizations of the observational data distribution. A point in the 2⁢σ2𝜎2\sigma2 italic_σ curve corresponds to the 95% quantile of the previous 300 realizations.

We stress the following results at 2⁢σ2𝜎2\sigma2 italic_σ level for the current and the forecast data: i𝑖iitalic_i) assuming mth=2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}=2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for the minimum BH mass: k<2.5𝑘2.5k<2.5italic_k < 2.5 (current) and k<1.6𝑘1.6k<1.6italic_k < 1.6 (forecast). i⁢i𝑖𝑖iiitalic_i italic_i) for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3, mth<1.1⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th1.1subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<1.1M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 1.1 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (current) and mth<0.3⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.3subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.3M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.3 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (forecast). For k=1𝑘1k=1italic_k = 1, there are no constraints from the current data and the forecast yields a very weak mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT constraint. For k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5, there are no constraints from this approach.

Finally, in Fig. 6 we plot the individual probabilities pisubscript𝑝𝑖p_{i}italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT as a function of the BH mass for the reference case. As expected, small BHs are more likely to originate from below-threshold masses. However, all values of pisubscript𝑝𝑖p_{i}italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are relatively close to unity (larger than 0.8), implying that currently observed BH are more likely to be formed above the threshold than below it. It is the combined probability, rather that some peculiar outlier, that leads to the conclusion that at least one BH should have been formed below threshold.

We find that, apart from a small dispersion that depends on the merging redshift (zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT), the probability p⁢(m)𝑝𝑚p(m)italic_p ( italic_m ) for a BH of observed mass m𝑚mitalic_m (in solar mass units) can be very well approximated in the reference case by the following function

p⁢(m)=C⁢log⁡(A−B⁢m−1/2),𝑝𝑚𝐶𝐴𝐵superscript𝑚12p(m)=C\log\big{(}A-Bm^{-1/2}\big{)}\,,italic_p ( italic_m ) = italic_C roman_log ( italic_A - italic_B italic_m start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 1 / 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , (13)

with A=26.72,B=30.88,C=0.3096formulae-sequence𝐴26.72formulae-sequence𝐵30.88𝐶0.3096A=26.72,B=30.88,C=0.3096italic_A = 26.72 , italic_B = 30.88 , italic_C = 0.3096. The form of this function is suggested by the analytical integration of Eq. (5) and Eq. (11) for a pure CDM model and a 1/td1subscript𝑡𝑑1/t_{d}1 / italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution; the coefficients are then obtained as a best fit to the actual pisubscript𝑝𝑖p_{i}italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values.

5 Constraints using the power-law-plus-peak distribution

5.1 General procedures

We now move to the PLPP method. The true population of merged BBHs is not well described by the detected BBHs since detection bias has an important role. In particular, it is known that it is easier to detect massive BBH systems than low-mass systems: many low-mass BBH mergers are expected to happen but are undetected.

A successful profile for the mass distribution of merged BBHs (i.e., after modelling and correcting the detection bias) is the power-law-plus-peak (PLPP) one, as proposed by Talbot & Thrane (2018) and analysed with current data (Abbott et al., 2021d, 2023). Considering the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT mass distribution, the PLPP is a combination of a power law, described by β⁢(m1)𝛽subscript𝑚1\beta(m_{1})italic_β ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ), a Gaussian peak given by G⁢(m1)𝐺subscript𝑚1G(m_{1})italic_G ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) and a smoothing function S⁢(m1)𝑆subscript𝑚1S(m_{1})italic_S ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) that smooths the minimum mass probability transition. The PLPP depends on seven parameters to describe the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution: the power α𝛼\alphaitalic_α, the minimum and maximum masses (mmin,mmaxsubscript𝑚minsubscript𝑚maxm_{\rm min},m_{\rm max}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT), the Gaussian mean and standard deviation (μ,σ𝜇𝜎\mu,\sigmaitalic_μ , italic_σ), the smoothing parameter δmsubscript𝛿𝑚\delta_{m}italic_δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and the λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ parameter that adjusts the relative importance of the peak and the Gaussian. The peak is interpreted as a consequence of pair-instability supernovae (Talbot & Thrane, 2018). The smoothing function S𝑆Sitalic_S is introduced since the most probable m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values are not expected to be at the minimum mminsubscript𝑚minm_{\rm min}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT: expectations from X-ray binaries and simulations (Talbot & Thrane, 2018) suggest a smoother transition. Explicitly, the PDF reads,

π⁢(m1)∝(1−λ)⁢β⁢(m1)⁢S⁢(m1)+λ⁢G⁢(m1)⁢S⁢(m1),proportional-to𝜋subscript𝑚11𝜆𝛽subscript𝑚1𝑆subscript𝑚1𝜆𝐺subscript𝑚1𝑆subscript𝑚1\displaystyle\pi(m_{1})\propto(1-\lambda)\beta(m_{1})S(m_{1})+\lambda G(m_{1})% S(m_{1})\,,italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ∝ ( 1 - italic_λ ) italic_β ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) italic_S ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) + italic_λ italic_G ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) italic_S ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ,
β⁢(m1)=α−1mmin1−α−mmax1−α⁢m1−α,𝛽subscript𝑚1𝛼1superscriptsubscript𝑚min1𝛼superscriptsubscript𝑚max1𝛼superscriptsubscript𝑚1𝛼\displaystyle\beta(m_{1})=\frac{\alpha-1}{m_{\rm min}^{1-\alpha}-m_{\rm max}^{% 1-\alpha}}m_{1}^{-\alpha},italic_β ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = divide start_ARG italic_α - 1 end_ARG start_ARG italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 1 - italic_α end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 1 - italic_α end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - italic_α end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ,
G⁢(m1)=12⁢π⁢σ⁢exp⁡(−(m1−μ)22⁢σ2),𝐺subscript𝑚112𝜋𝜎superscriptsubscript𝑚1𝜇22superscript𝜎2\displaystyle G(m_{1})=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma}\exp\left(-\frac{(m_{1}-% \mu)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)\,,italic_G ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = divide start_ARG 1 end_ARG start_ARG square-root start_ARG 2 italic_π end_ARG italic_σ end_ARG roman_exp ( - divide start_ARG ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic_μ ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG 2 italic_σ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG ) , (14)
S⁢(m1)={[1+exp⁡(δmδ⁢m1−δmδ⁢m1−δm)]−1,δm1<δm1,δm1>δm,\displaystyle S(m_{1})=\begin{cases}\left[1+\exp\left(\frac{\delta_{m}}{\delta m% _{1}}-\frac{\delta_{m}}{\delta m_{1}-\delta_{m}}\right)\right]^{-1}&\!\!\!\!\!% \!,\,\delta m_{1}<\delta_{m}\\[14.22636pt] 1&\!\!\!\!\!\!,\,\delta m_{1}>\delta_{m}\end{cases}\,,italic_S ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = { start_ROW start_CELL [ 1 + roman_exp ( divide start_ARG italic_δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG italic_δ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG - divide start_ARG italic_δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG italic_δ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic_δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) ] start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_CELL start_CELL , italic_δ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_CELL end_ROW start_ROW start_CELL 1 end_CELL start_CELL , italic_δ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT > italic_δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_CELL end_ROW ,

where δ⁢m1≡m1−mmin𝛿subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚min\delta m_{1}\equiv m_{1}-m_{\rm min}italic_δ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≡ italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. It is also imposed that π⁢(m1)=0𝜋subscript𝑚10\pi(m_{1})=0italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = 0 for m1<mminsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚minm_{1}<m_{\rm min}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT or m1>mmaxsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚maxm_{1}>m_{\rm max}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT > italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. The PLPP parameter mminsubscript𝑚minm_{\rm min}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT states the minimum mass for both m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses. It should not be interpreted as stating the minimum mass of any BH, it is a fitted parameter that best describes the BBH merging population assuming that the PLPP profile holds. If this parameter is decreased, lower masses for the merging BBH population are possible; hence, in the CCBH context, even lower masses are necessary at formation time. Lowering mminsubscript𝑚minm_{\rm min}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT increases the constraints we find, while larger mminsubscript𝑚minm_{\rm min}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values will decrease them. There is an analogous behaviour in the direct approach, in the sense that, the lower is the detected mass at merger time, the stronger will be our constraints.

The parameters are found from GW observational data and considering the detector bias, through a hierarchical Bayesian approach (Abbott et al., 2023). The PLPP model represents the source frame mass distribution corrected for the selection effects. For the GWTC-3 data, the eight parameters that describe the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distributions are (LIGO Scientific Collaboration et al., 2023) (90% credible intervals):

α=3.40−0.49+0.58,𝛼superscriptsubscript3.400.490.58\displaystyle\alpha=3.40_{-0.49}^{+0.58},italic_α = 3.40 start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 0.49 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 0.58 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , δm=4.8−3.2+3.3⁢M⊙,subscript𝛿𝑚superscriptsubscript4.83.23.3subscriptMdirect-product\displaystyle\delta_{m}=4.8_{-3.2}^{+3.3}\,{\rm M}_{\odot},italic_δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 4.8 start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 3.2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 3.3 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ,
mmin=5.08−1.5+0.87⁢M⊙,subscript𝑚minsuperscriptsubscript5.081.50.87subscriptMdirect-product\displaystyle m_{\rm min}=5.08_{-1.5}^{+0.87}\,{\rm M}_{\odot},\;\;italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 5.08 start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 1.5 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 0.87 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , mmax=86.9−9.4+11.⁢M⊙,subscript𝑚maxsuperscriptsubscript86.99.411subscriptMdirect-product\displaystyle m_{\rm max}=86.9_{-9.4}^{+11.}\,{\rm M}_{\odot},italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 86.9 start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 9.4 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 11 . end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , (15)
μ=33.7−3.8+2.3⁢M⊙,𝜇superscriptsubscript33.73.82.3subscriptMdirect-product\displaystyle\mu=33.7_{-3.8}^{+2.3}\,{\rm M}_{\odot},italic_μ = 33.7 start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 3.8 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 2.3 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , σ=3.6−2.1+4.6⁢M⊙,𝜎superscriptsubscript3.62.14.6subscriptMdirect-product\displaystyle\sigma=3.6_{-2.1}^{+4.6}\,{\rm M}_{\odot}\,,italic_σ = 3.6 start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 2.1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 4.6 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ,
λ=0.039−0.026+0.058,𝜆superscriptsubscript0.0390.0260.058\displaystyle\lambda=0.039_{-0.026}^{+0.058}\,,italic_λ = 0.039 start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 0.026 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 0.058 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , βq=1.1−1.3+1.8.subscript𝛽𝑞superscriptsubscript1.11.31.8\displaystyle\beta_{q}=1.1_{-1.3}^{+1.8}\,.italic_β start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_q end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1.1 start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 1.3 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 1.8 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT .

The βqsubscript𝛽𝑞\beta_{q}italic_β start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_q end_POSTSUBSCRIPT parameter, only needed to describe the m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution, will be discussed later on. The central values above are the medians of the posteriors, while the uncertainties represent the 5%percent55\%5 % and 90%percent9090\%90 % quantiles of the posteriors. From (LIGO Scientific Collaboration et al., 2023) we also infer the maximum likelihood values, which read,

α=3.55,mmin=4.82⁢M⊙,mmax=83.14⁢M⊙,formulae-sequence𝛼3.55formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚min4.82subscriptMdirect-productsubscript𝑚max83.14subscriptMdirect-product\displaystyle\alpha=3.55,\;m_{\rm min}=4.82{\rm M}_{\odot},\;m_{\rm max}=83.14% {\rm M}_{\odot}\,,italic_α = 3.55 , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 4.82 roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 83.14 roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ,
δm=5.45⁢M⊙,μ=34.47⁢M⊙,σ=1.87⁢M⊙,formulae-sequencesubscript𝛿𝑚5.45subscriptMdirect-productformulae-sequence𝜇34.47subscriptMdirect-product𝜎1.87subscriptMdirect-product\displaystyle\delta_{m}=5.45{\rm M}_{\odot},\;\mu=34.47{\rm M}_{\odot},\;% \sigma=1.87{\rm M}_{\odot}\,,italic_δ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 5.45 roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_μ = 34.47 roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_σ = 1.87 roman_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , (16)
λ=0.019,βq=0.76.formulae-sequence𝜆0.019subscript𝛽𝑞0.76\displaystyle\lambda=0.019,\;\beta_{q}=0.76\,.italic_λ = 0.019 , italic_β start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_q end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.76 .

To test the CCBH hypothesis, we use the merging BBH distribution, as provided by PLPP, to find the expected BBH mass distribution by the formation time, as illustrated in Fig. 7. More precisely, let M1,msubscript𝑀1𝑚M_{1,m}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 , italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT be a random realization of the PLPP distribution, where the index m𝑚mitalic_m stands for merger time, and let Fzmsubscript𝐹subscript𝑧𝑚F_{z_{m}}italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT be a random realization of the mass factor correction of eq. (8) at given zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Then the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution at BBH formation time is the distribution of the random variate M1,isubscript𝑀1𝑖M_{1,i}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 , italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, with

M1,i=Fzm⁢M1,m.subscript𝑀1𝑖subscript𝐹subscript𝑧𝑚subscript𝑀1𝑚M_{1,i}=F_{z_{m}}M_{1,m}\,.italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 , italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_F start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 , italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT . (17)

Our results are found using at least 105superscript10510^{5}10 start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 5 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT realizations of each random variable.

A caveat of the above procedure is that the mass factor distribution depends on zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, hence the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution by formation time is zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT dependent. This dependence on zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT can be either considered by using the zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values from observations or by using a mean zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT value as an approximation. The dependence on zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is weak (see also Fig. 6), thus simply using an average zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT value is sufficient. The quality of the approximation can be directly verified by computing any probability at the minimum and maximum zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values.

From the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT mass distribution at the BBH formation, one can compute what is the probability that one of the merged BBHs could have been formed with m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT larger than a given mass threshold mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, denoted by p⁢(mth,zm)𝑝subscript𝑚thsubscript𝑧𝑚p(m_{\rm th},z_{m})italic_p ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ). For the various scenarios studied here, this probability for a single event with a mass larger than 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is not far from, but clearly below, unity. Since the total number of merged BBHs is larger than the total number of confidently detected mergers, a minimum bound can be found by using the confidently detected BHs from gravitational waves (denoted by N𝑁Nitalic_N), which we will use here. Hence, the probability of a given CCBH realization being compatible with existing data, similarly to eq. (12), is

P⁢(m1>mth)=∏jNpj⁢(mth,zm,j)≈pN⁢(mth,zm¯),𝑃subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚thsuperscriptsubscriptproduct𝑗𝑁subscript𝑝𝑗subscript𝑚thsubscript𝑧𝑚𝑗superscript𝑝𝑁subscript𝑚th¯subscript𝑧𝑚P(m_{1}>m_{\rm th})=\prod_{j}^{N}p_{j}(m_{\rm th},z_{m,j})\approx p^{N}(m_{\rm th% },\overline{z_{m}})\,,italic_P ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT > italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = ∏ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m , italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ≈ italic_p start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , over¯ start_ARG italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) , (18)

where zm¯¯subscript𝑧𝑚\overline{z_{m}}over¯ start_ARG italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG is an average over all the zm,jsubscript𝑧𝑚𝑗z_{m,j}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m , italic_j end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values. The above equation can be computed either using the redshift values of the observed merged BBH or by using the last approximation, which only depends on zm¯¯subscript𝑧𝑚\overline{z_{m}}over¯ start_ARG italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG. The numerical differences are small, being negligible when stating the tension with σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ units.

The above analysis applies for m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses. Following Talbot & Thrane (2018), the m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution is given by the following conditional probability,

π⁢(m2|m1)∝(m2m1)βq⁢S⁢(m2)⁢Θ⁢(m⁢1−m2),proportional-to𝜋conditionalsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1superscriptsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1subscript𝛽𝑞𝑆subscript𝑚2Θ𝑚1subscript𝑚2\pi(m_{2}|m_{1})\propto\left(\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}\right)^{\beta_{q}}S(m_{2})% \Theta(m1-m_{2})\,,italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT | italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ∝ ( divide start_ARG italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_β start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_q end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_S ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) roman_Θ ( italic_m 1 - italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) , (19)

where βqsubscript𝛽𝑞\beta_{q}italic_β start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_q end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the single PLPP parameter that only appears in the m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution, S𝑆Sitalic_S is the smoothing function as defined in (14) and ΘΘ\Thetaroman_Θ is the Heaviside theta function. In order to find the PDF π⁢(m2)𝜋subscript𝑚2\pi(m_{2})italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) we marginalize over m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT,

π⁢(m2)=∫mminmmaxπ⁢(m2|m1)⁢π⁢(m1)⁢d⁢m1,𝜋subscript𝑚2superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑚minsubscript𝑚max𝜋conditionalsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1𝜋subscript𝑚1dsubscript𝑚1\pi(m_{2})=\int_{m_{\rm min}}^{m_{\rm max}}\pi(m_{2}|m_{1})\pi(m_{1})\textrm{d% }m_{1}\,,italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT | italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) d italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , (20)

where π⁢(m1)𝜋subscript𝑚1\pi(m_{1})italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is given in eq. (14) and π⁢(m2|m1)𝜋conditionalsubscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1\pi(m_{2}|m_{1})italic_π ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT | italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) needs to be normalized for each m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT value. With this result, one can find the initial mass distribution and compute the probability P⁢(m1>mth)𝑃subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚thP(m_{1}>m_{\rm th})italic_P ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT > italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ), eq. (18), for m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses.

5.2 Results

Using the approach illustrated in Fig. 7, in Fig. 8 we show m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distributions at formation time. Different values of k𝑘kitalic_k and of the three parameters related to the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution (tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, tminsubscript𝑡mint_{\rm min}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, zmaxsubscript𝑧maxz_{\rm max}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) are considered. Changing the latter three parameters can reduce the probability of a BH formation with mass below 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, but, as long as this PDF is not negligible for m<2⁢M⊙𝑚2subscript𝑀direct-productm<2M_{\odot}italic_m < 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, the tension between observational data and the minimum BH mass will increase with the number of detected BHs. This figure also shows that the k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5 case, which was specially studied by Croker et al. (2021) as a GBH model, is the safest among the shown cases in this figure.

By applying the mass factor correction on the PLPP distribution (17), with the best-fit parameters (16), and using eq. (18) with N=72𝑁72N=72italic_N = 72, the probability that no merged BBH was formed with mass smaller than 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is P≈2×10−4𝑃2superscript104P\approx 2\times 10^{-4}italic_P ≈ 2 × 10 start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 4 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, thus implying a minimum tension of 3.7σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ for the reference values. With new detections in the current LVK run (O4), assuming that the PLPP profile with the best parameter values and the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution will remain the same, the tension is forecast to increase beyond 5⁢σ5𝜎5\sigma5 italic_σ (250 events).

Instead of m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, one may consider the m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses, which lead to stronger constraints but depend on an additional PLPP parameter, βqsubscript𝛽𝑞\beta_{q}italic_β start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_q end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. In our events selection, Table 1, there are 69 m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT masses larger than 5⁢M⊙5subscript𝑀direct-product5M_{\odot}5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT: these are too massive to be NSs. Using the m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution, eq. (20), we find that the tension becomes 4.0σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ for mth=2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}=2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Fixing k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3 but reducing the minimum mass, one finds that the tension disappears (i.e., less than 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ) if mth<0.5⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.5subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.5M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. These results assume the best-fit PLPP parameters. In the following, we return to focus on the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT analysis.

In Fig. 9 we show how the tension changes with the BH minimum mass, considering m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT data. This figure also considers changes in w𝑤witalic_w, in the context of the DEBH model. The tension decreases for larger w𝑤witalic_w values, which implies lower k𝑘kitalic_k values. It is important to point out that the case k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5 is safe (Croker et al., 2021): the forecast result implies no tension, as expected, since the modified PLPP profile at formation time, Fig. 8, show no relevant probability for m1<2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚12subscript𝑀direct-productm_{1}<2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT.

In Fig. 10 we show exclusion plots for the DEBH and GBH models considering parameter variations with respect to the reference values (for the 72 observed m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT data), fixed PLPP parameters as the best values and mþ=2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚italic-þ2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\th}=2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_þ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. All the reference value changes here considered are such that the tension decreases. This figure should be compared with Fig. 2. Both figures show qualitatively the same behaviour, but quantitatively, as expected, the PLPP approach is stronger. We are thus left with only four parameters: k𝑘kitalic_k, tminsubscript𝑡mint_{\rm min}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and zmaxsubscript𝑧maxz_{\rm max}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. We see that, out of these four, the two most important ones which could be changed to alleviate the tension are k𝑘kitalic_k (as expected) and tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. However, we do not have a physical explanation for using a low tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT value (that would require an unexpected restriction on the possible initial conditions of BBHs). For the DEBH model, the tension is reduced by increasing w𝑤witalic_w, which corresponds to decreasing k𝑘kitalic_k, but even the case w=−0.6𝑤0.6w=-0.6italic_w = - 0.6, which is far from the standard cosmological model, is not sufficient to drop the tension to an acceptable level. In the following, using Fig. 10, we highlight the necessary individual parameter ranges that reduce the reference tension from 3.7σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ to an acceptable level, below 2.0σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ. They are: k≤1.7𝑘1.7k\leq 1.7italic_k ≤ 1.7, tmax<6subscript𝑡max6t_{\rm max}<6italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 6 Gyr, zmax<2subscript𝑧max2z_{\rm max}<2italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 2. Reducing tminsubscript𝑡mint_{\rm min}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT from 50 Myr to 5 Myr only marginally improves the picture. Although imposing a strong bound on the maximum delay time value is effective at reducing the tension, there is no clear physical mechanism for this. In principle, BBHs are formed with a large spread on the starting orbit and masses. In the standard picture (k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0), most of them need not to merge within a Hubble time (e.g., Ghodla et al., 2023).

In Fig. 11 we consider how the m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT-based CCBH tension changes by considering 103superscript10310^{3}10 start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT samples of the PLPP parameter distribution (LIGO Scientific Collaboration et al., 2023) and mþ=2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚italic-þ2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\th}=2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_þ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. We stress the following properties: i𝑖iitalic_i) For the k=1𝑘1k=1italic_k = 1 case, the results for the current and forecast data are compatible with no tension (i.e., below 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ); i⁢i𝑖𝑖iiitalic_i italic_i) for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3 case with current data, 95% of the realizations have tension larger than 3.2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ; i⁢i⁢i𝑖𝑖𝑖iiiitalic_i italic_i italic_i) for the forecast with k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3, 95% of the realizations have tension larger than 6⁢σ6𝜎6\sigma6 italic_σ. iv)iv)italic_i italic_v ) Although the spread for the PLPP method is larger than for the direct one, the PLPP results are clearly stronger for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3.

Figure 12, similarly to Fig. 5, explores the combined variation of the two most relevant parameters for this analysis, k𝑘kitalic_k and minimum BH mass (mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT), and takes into account the full distribution of the PLPP parameters (instead of only using the best-value parameters). The main result is the blue solid line that delimits the 2⁢σ2𝜎2\sigma2 italic_σ excluded region for the current data. To find this curve we proceed as follows: for each pair of values (k,mth)𝑘subscript𝑚th(k,m_{\rm th})( italic_k , italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) and a particular realization of the PLPP parameters distribution, we compute the probability that there is no BH with mass below mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. We repeat the previous evaluation for 300 realizations of the observational data distribution. A point in the 2⁢σ2𝜎2\sigma2 italic_σ curve corresponds to the 95% quantile of the previous 300 realizations.

We stress the following results at 2⁢σ2𝜎2\sigma2 italic_σ level for the current and the forecast data: i𝑖iitalic_i) assuming mth=2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}=2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for the minimum BH mass: k<2.1𝑘2.1k<2.1italic_k < 2.1 (current) and k<1.4𝑘1.4k<1.4italic_k < 1.4 (forecast). i⁢i𝑖𝑖iiitalic_i italic_i) for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3, mth<0.8⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.8subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.8M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.8 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (current) and mth<0.2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (forecast). For k=1𝑘1k=1italic_k = 1, there are no constraints from the current data and the forecast yields mth<3.4⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th3.4subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<3.4M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 3.4 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. For k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5, there are no constraints.

6 Modified delay-time distribution from CCBH physics

In a recent work, Ghodla et al. (2023) (henceforth G23) considered the mass increase of CCBHs in BBHs systems. As discussed in Appendix B, even if individual delay times are significantly different due to the CCBH correction, this does not necessarily imply a change of the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution. Here we evaluate in detail the consequences within the picture described by G23, showing that this picture indeed leads to changes in the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution, but such changes can only strengthen the constraints we found from the log-uniform case. In the Appendix B, the reason for this behaviour is further explored. G23 find an explicit relation between the delay time of CCBHs with k>0𝑘0k>0italic_k > 0, denoted as tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and an auxiliary delay time that corresponds to the case without cosmological coupling (k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0), here denoted by t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. As pointed out by Croker & Weiner (2019); Croker et al. (2021), there are two factors that are important for the BBH dynamics and that are not part of the standard BH picture (k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0), namely: i𝑖iitalic_i) the emission of GWs becomes stronger with time, since the system is increasing its mass and, i⁢i𝑖𝑖iiitalic_i italic_i) conservation of orbital angular momentum. For a given BBH with a given initial orbit at formation, these two factors make tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT smaller than the corresponding t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. The point i𝑖iitalic_i above is quite clear and intuitive (since more massive binary systems should emit more GWs), the second point is not as obvious as it may seem, and we briefly review this subject.

The issue with angular momentum conservation can be traced to the question: as a CCBH increases its mass proportionally to aksuperscript𝑎𝑘a^{k}italic_a start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, should its velocity continue the same as if it had no cosmological mass increase? Unless further details in the microphysics are specified, one cannot present a definitive answer, because it depends on how a CCBH acquires mass and how it interacts with the rest of the universe. This is beyond the possible cosmological-fluid description of CCBHs since it is about the property of a single BH. The DEBH and GBH pictures need further input to fix this issue. Although imposing momentum conservation can sound like the simplest choice, this, together with the mass increase, leads to preferred-frame effects.
