Locally, the mass increase violates the particle 4-momentum conservation [pμ⁢pμ=−m2⁢(t)superscript𝑝𝜇subscript𝑝𝜇superscript𝑚2𝑡p^{\mu}p_{\mu}=-m^{2}(t)italic_p start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_μ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_μ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = - italic_m start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_t )]; and if 3-momentum (or spatial angular-momentum) is imposed to be conserved while pμsuperscript𝑝𝜇p^{\mu}italic_p start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_μ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT is not conserved, there is a break of boost invariance, since only p0subscript𝑝0p_{0}italic_p start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT will not be conserved. This is not a problem in itself: since CCBHs are interacting with the universe, their preferred frame could be the CMB frame, for instance, but this is an additional ingredient to be specified (see also, Avelino, 2023, for a related discussion on prefered frames). Moreover, the case with momentum conservation is the one with the strongest phenomenological bounds (see G23). Henceforth we only consider the case without angular momentum conservation. If the latter is imposed, constraints can only become stronger for any given k𝑘kitalic_k.

G23 finds that, for a BBH system without eccentricity, the evolution of the orbit radius r𝑟ritalic_r about the center of mass follows the same corresponding equation as for standard BHs, apart from a correcting factor (with c=1𝑐1c=1italic_c = 1),

d⁢rd⁢t=−645⁢G3⁢μ⁢M2r3⁢(aai)3⁢k,𝑑𝑟𝑑𝑡645superscript𝐺3𝜇superscript𝑀2superscript𝑟3superscript𝑎subscript𝑎𝑖3𝑘\frac{dr}{dt}=-\frac{64}{5}\frac{G^{3}\mu M^{2}}{r^{3}}\left(\frac{a}{a_{i}}% \right)^{3k}\,,divide start_ARG italic_d italic_r end_ARG start_ARG italic_d italic_t end_ARG = - divide start_ARG 64 end_ARG start_ARG 5 end_ARG divide start_ARG italic_G start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_μ italic_M start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG italic_r start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_ARG ( divide start_ARG italic_a end_ARG start_ARG italic_a start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , (21)

where M=m1+m2𝑀subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2M=m_{1}+m_{2}italic_M = italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT + italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and μ=m1⁢m2/(m1+m2)𝜇subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2\mu=m_{1}m_{2}/(m_{1}+m_{2})italic_μ = italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / ( italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT + italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ). Terms that depend on a𝑎aitalic_a derivatives are negligible. By solving the differential equation above for r⁢(t)𝑟𝑡r(t)italic_r ( italic_t ) and using r⁢(tm)=0𝑟subscript𝑡𝑚0r(t_{m})=0italic_r ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = 0 and tm=td+tisubscript𝑡𝑚subscript𝑡dsubscript𝑡𝑖t_{m}=t_{\rm d}+t_{i}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT + italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, where tmsubscript𝑡𝑚t_{m}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the merger time and tisubscript𝑡𝑖t_{i}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT the initial or formation time, one finds

∫titd+ti(a⁢(t)ai)3⁢k⁢𝑑t=t~d.subscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑡dsubscript𝑡𝑖subscript𝑡𝑖superscript𝑎𝑡subscript𝑎𝑖3𝑘differential-d𝑡subscript~𝑡d\int^{t_{\rm d}+t_{i}}_{t_{i}}\left(\frac{a(t)}{a_{i}}\right)^{3k}dt=\tilde{t}% _{\rm d}\,.∫ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT + italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( divide start_ARG italic_a ( italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG italic_a start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_d italic_t = over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT . (22)

This is the relation between the delay times of CCBH and the delay times of standard BHs (see G23). This relation does not impose momentum conservation, otherwise the exponent 3⁢k3𝑘3k3 italic_k should be replaced by 15⁢k15𝑘15k15 italic_k.

Equation (22) provides the value of tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for given tisubscript𝑡𝑖t_{i}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (assuming a⁢(t)𝑎𝑡a(t)italic_a ( italic_t ), k𝑘kitalic_k and t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are given). Actually, we need tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for given zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, because the starting point of our analysis is the observational data, not the initial configuration. Hence we simply write,

∫tm−tdtm(a⁢(t)ai)3⁢k⁢𝑑t=t~d.subscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑡𝑚subscript𝑡𝑚subscript𝑡dsuperscript𝑎𝑡subscript𝑎𝑖3𝑘differential-d𝑡subscript~𝑡d\int^{t_{m}}_{t_{m}-t_{\rm d}}\left(\frac{a(t)}{a_{i}}\right)^{3k}dt=\tilde{t}% _{\rm d}\,.∫ start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( divide start_ARG italic_a ( italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG italic_a start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 3 italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_d italic_t = over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT . (23)

Using our reference values for tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, eq. (3), it is possible to plot the relation above for different values of the merging redshift zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. This is displayed in the left panel of Fig. 13. As can be seen in this panel, in the small tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT region, a given t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT interval is mapped into a tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT interval with approximately the same length. However, for large tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values, a wide interval in the t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT axis corresponds to a much narrower interval in the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT one. This means that the probability of large tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values is increased with respect to the t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution. The tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution for different zmsubscript𝑧𝑚z_{m}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values is shown in detail in the right plot of the same figure.

Fig. 14 combines the direct and PLPP 2⁢σ2𝜎2\sigma2 italic_σ bounds on the k𝑘kitalic_k and the minimum BH mass constraints, it should be compared with Figs. 5 and 12. These bounds consider the uncertainties in the observational data and on the PLPP parameters. The constraints are much stronger using the G23 relation. We stress here the same particular cases that were considered before: i𝑖iitalic_i) assuming mth=2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}=2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT for the minimum BH mass: k<1.3𝑘1.3k<1.3italic_k < 1.3 (direct), k<1.1𝑘1.1k<1.1italic_k < 1.1 (PLPP). i⁢i𝑖𝑖iiitalic_i italic_i) for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3, mth<0.2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (direct), mth<0.1⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.1subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.1M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.1 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (PLPP). For k=1𝑘1k=1italic_k = 1, mth<3.3⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th3.3subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<3.3M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 3.3 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (direct), mth<2.3⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th2.3subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<2.3M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 2.3 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (PLPP). For k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5, there are no constraints.

7 Conclusions

According to a recent proposal (Croker & Weiner, 2019; Farrah et al., 2023b), BHs grow in mass due to a “cosmological coupling” and might be responsible for the cosmic acceleration. In such a scenario, dubbed cosmologically coupled BHs, or CCBH, BHs are not of the Kerr type, and are supposed to match asymptotically the cosmological background. This bold idea seems supported by recent analyses of the growth of supermassive BHs in quiescent elliptical galaxies (Farrah et al., 2023a).

In this paper we tested this hypothesis by considering the binary BHs with stellar progenitors observed with gravitational waves by the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA (LVK) detectors. If these BHs are cosmologically coupled, they should have undergone a fast mass growth correlated with the cosmological scale factor from the moment of their formation until merging, and should have formed therefore with a mass smaller than the merger one. Since according to the current understanding there is a mass limit below which stellar BHs cannot form, the presently observed masses could be in conflict with this mass threshold. To compute this conflict, one needs to know the time between the BBH formation and its merger, which is the delay time (tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT). The standard tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution, without cosmological coupling, is the log-uniform one (Abbott et al., 2021c, 2023). It is not established what the CCBH impact on the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution would be, since it depends on unknown microphysical details (it may preserve the log-uniform or not). Our main analysis here is based on either the log-uniform distribution or the CCBH-corrected approach of Ghodla et al. (2023). The latter increases our constraints since it favours larger delay times, thus increasing the gap between the formation and the merged mass. This seems to be a general feature of CCBH-corrections to the standard tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution, as discussed in Appendix B. At last, we also study the consequences of a general power-law tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution (Appendix C).

We developed two methods of analysis, one is directly based on the observed events only (direct method), and the other is based on the merged BBH population distribution taking into account observational bias, which uses the power-law-plus-peak distribution (PLPP method). The PLPP distribution is the standard model for the merged population of BBHs. Once the uncertainties of each method are taken into account, the results are not far from each other. The PLPP approach yields the strongest constraints.

The main result is that the combination of a minimum BH mass with the GWs data from binary compact objects can put strong bounds on the CCBH approach with current data. For k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3 and minimum BH mass (mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) of 2 M⊙subscript𝑀direct-productM_{\odot}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, a tension of 3⁢σ3𝜎3\sigma3 italic_σ or more is found with the current data. Moreover, we show that such bounds can quickly be confirmed with new data from LVK and that CCBH corrections to the delay-time distribution commonly enhance the constraints. Specifically, with the current m1subscript𝑚1m_{1}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT data, for mth=2⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}=2M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT: k<2.5⁢(1.3)𝑘2.51.3k<2.5(1.3)italic_k < 2.5 ( 1.3 ), for the direct method, and k<2.1⁢(1.1)𝑘2.11.1k<2.1(1.1)italic_k < 2.1 ( 1.1 ) for the PLPP method, at 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ level. The values in parenthesis use the Ghodla et al. (2023) delay-time correction. Considering the uncertainties on the nature of CCBHs, we also find the required mthsubscript𝑚thm_{\rm th}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT value to eliminate the tensions for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3, and find mth<0.5⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.5subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.5M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. This is a remarkably low value for a BH-like object of stellar origin, as discussed in Appendix A. Finally, for the CCBH variation studied by Croker et al. (2021), whose BH masses increase more slowly (k=0.5𝑘0.5k=0.5italic_k = 0.5), we found no relevant tension with minimum BH masses. This conclusion is also valid for the case k=1𝑘1k=1italic_k = 1, which was recently studied by Cadoni et al. (2023b, a).

At last, we stress that If the LVK BHs are of primordial origin, then a completely different analysis would be needed (see also Ghodla et al., 2023).

Acknowledgements

We thank Kevin Croker and Valerio Faraoni for very useful comments and feedback. We also thank Riccardo Sturani for several discussions about this project. LA acknowledges support from DFG project 456622116. DCR thanks Heidelberg University for hospitality and support, he also acknowledges support from Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq-Brazil) and Fundação de Amparo à Pesquisa e Inovação do Espírito Santo (FAPES-Brazil) (TO 1020/2022, 976/2022, 1081/2022). MQ is supported by the Brazilian research agencies FAPERJ, CNPq (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico) and CAPES. This study was financed in part by the Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior - Brasil (CAPES) - Finance Code 001. We acknowledge support from the CAPES-DAAD bilateral project “Data Analysis and Model Testing in the Era of Precision Cosmology”.

Data Availability

The data underlying this article are available in the article, in its online supplementary table and in the codes https://github.com/itpamendola/CCBH-direct and https://github.com/davi-rodrigues/CCBH-Numerics.

  • van Son et al. (2022) van Son L. A. C., et al., 2022, Astrophys. J., 931, 17

Appendix A CCBH and minimum mass

Here we discuss the adopted 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT as the main value for the minimum BH mass in the CCBH context. We also add considerations about possible future confirmations of low mass BHs.

Neutron star (NS) stability studies based on the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff (TOV) equation predict that nonrotating NS could have masses at least as high as 2.2⁢M⊙2.2subscript𝑀direct-product2.2M_{\odot}2.2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Ye & Fishbach, 2022; Legred et al., 2021), which sets a lower bound for Kerr BHs forming through stellar collapse (this bound need not be satisfied for general horizonless compact objects). Incidentally, rotating NS have been measured with masses as high as 2.7⁢M⊙2.7subscript𝑀direct-product2.7M_{\odot}2.7 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Romani et al., 2012), and studies of binary systems containing NS find an empirical upper limit as high as 2.6⁢M⊙2.6subscript𝑀direct-product2.6M_{\odot}2.6 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (Rocha et al., 2021). This constraint of 2.2⁢M⊙2.2subscript𝑀direct-product2.2M_{\odot}2.2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT was used in the main results of Rodriguez (2023) and Andrae & El-Badry (2023).

Since GBHs simply grow proportionally to aksuperscript𝑎𝑘a^{k}italic_a start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, they may or may not have a horizon. On the other hand, DEBHs (which are a type of GEODE, Croker & Weiner, 2019) need be a source of DE, thus such objects are not expected to have a horizon. They could be named non-singular BHs or exotic compact objects (ECOs) (Mazur & Mottola, 2015; Cardoso & Pani, 2019). The limit of 2.2⁢M⊙2.2subscript𝑀direct-product2.2M_{\odot}2.2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT need not to apply to them (e.g., Croker & Weiner, 2019; Farrah et al., 2023b; Rodriguez, 2023).

Although non-singular BHs may form from the stellar evolution (e.g., Mazur & Mottola, 2015, 2023) and they may avoid the above horizon-based mass bound, they need to be compatible with the observed NSs with masses as high as ∼2.5⁢M⊙similar-toabsent2.5subscript𝑀direct-product\sim 2.5M_{\odot}∼ 2.5 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, as above mentioned. BH-like objects with masses about one solar mass are not commonly expected to be of stellar origin. For instance, Abbott et al. (2022) study the possible detection of such objects with LVK, but they are considered to be either of primordial cosmological origin (PBHs) or arising from the collapse of dark matter (which is possible for some particular dark matter models). In the literature on BHs and NSs, there is a large discussion on the mass gap that separates the maximum non-rotating NSs masses from the minimum detected BH ones. At least currently, there is no support for the generation of stellar BHs with about one solar mass or below. Considering the current scenario, 2⁢M⊙2subscript𝑀direct-product2M_{\odot}2 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is a conservative minimum BH mass (i.e., lower than other standard limits), even in the DEBH scenario, considering the lack of a microphysical theory to generate such low mass BHs from stellar evolution.444Gao & Li (2023) consider k>0𝑘0k>0italic_k > 0 to explain the mass gap. Nonetheless, we also do several specific tests with other minimum mass values.

We consider now the consequences of a low mass BH detection. If observed BHs are assumed to be a primordial one, then our analysis would not apply, since the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution should be considerably different. We only consider BHs of stellar origin. Morras et al. (2023) present moderate significance evidence for a subsolar compact object detection of mass m2=0.76−0.14+0.50⁢M⊙subscript𝑚2subscriptsuperscript0.760.500.14subscript𝑀direct-productm_{2}=0.76^{+0.50}_{-0.14}M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.76 start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT + 0.50 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT - 0.14 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT at zm=0.028subscript𝑧𝑚0.028z_{m}=0.028italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.028 (it is the low mass companion of the binary system). This is not a strong evidence, since there are still 16% of chance of its mass being above 1 M⊙subscript𝑀direct-productM_{\odot}italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and hence that it could be a NS. Nonetheless, the consequences of a confirmation would be remarkable. Possibly this can be achieved with either the next LVK run or the next generation of GW detectors, as the Einstein Telescope (e.g., Wolfe et al., 2023). If such a small mass compact object exists and is indeed a BH-like object of stellar origin, it is possible to infer an upper bound on the minimum BH mass value using our methods. Here we only consider the direct method, since we are only adding a new event and we are not assuming that we know the population of merging low mass BHs.

For comparison, we consider two sets of m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT data. The first set is composed by all the 72 m2subscript𝑚2m_{2}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT events listed in Table 1, apart from the two least massive ones, which are assumed to be NSs by LVK. For this set, the least massive BH has a mass of 2.6⁢M⊙2.6subscript𝑀direct-product2.6M_{\odot}2.6 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. The second set is composed by the previous data plus the Morras et al. (2023) object, with mass 0.76⁢M⊙0.76subscript𝑀direct-product0.76M_{\odot}0.76 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT.

For the first data set, and for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3, the minimum BH mass threshold should satisfy mth<0.73⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.73subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.73\,M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.73 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT at 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ level. Once the Morras et al. (2023) object is considered, one finds mth<0.57⁢M⊙subscript𝑚th0.57subscript𝑀direct-productm_{\rm th}<0.57\,M_{\odot}italic_m start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_th end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < 0.57 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Figure 15 shows further details. In this figure, as the threshold becomes closer to the detected BH mass, the probability quickly goes to zero.

The above application shows that, as expected, the less massive the detected BH, the smaller should be the minimal CCBH mass to avoid tension with the observational data. To compute a rejection level for given k𝑘kitalic_k, one needs a reasonable theoretical estimate for a minimum stellar BH mass. For the particular case considered above, if such estimate is below 0.57⁢M⊙0.57subscript𝑀direct-product0.57M_{\odot}0.57 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, there would be no tension with the current data for k=3𝑘3k=3italic_k = 3 (neglecting detection bias).

The delay time distribution has a central role for the constraints we find. The next two appendices are devoted to the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution.

Appendix B CCBH-modified delay-time distributions compared with the log-uniform one: general approach

Here we consider the impact of CCBH on the delay-time distribution under general considerations that only depend on certain basic qualitative expectations. We show that the CCBH correction to the delay time distribution is not expected to decrease the chances of finding larger tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values with respect to the log-uniform distribution. It is shown that the particular correction that preserves the log-uniform distribution is a power-law correction, while exponential corrections and polynomials will increase the likelihood of high tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values. In Sec. 6 the particular realization studied by Ghodla et al. (2023) is considered.

Considering CCBHs as true for some fixed k𝑘kitalic_k value (0<k≤30𝑘30<k\leq 30 < italic_k ≤ 3), and for a given BBH configuration with given initial masses and initial orbit, one can in principle compute both the expected physical delay time tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT within this CCBH framework and an auxiliary delay time t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT that ignores the cosmological coupling effects (k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0). These delay times are expected to satisfy td<t~dsubscript𝑡dsubscript~𝑡d{t}_{\rm d}<\tilde{t}_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT since the cosmological coupling increases the BHs masses (thus it reduces the time needed from BBH formation up to the merger). More precisely, we will consider the following hypotheses that naturally emerge from this setting: i𝑖iitalic_i) there exists a function T𝑇Titalic_T that maps physical delay times into auxiliary ones, and this function has an inverse: t~d=T⁢(td)subscript~𝑡d𝑇subscript𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}=T(t_{\rm d})over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_T ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) and td=T−1⁢(t~d)subscript𝑡dsuperscript𝑇1subscript~𝑡dt_{\rm d}=T^{-1}(\tilde{t}_{\rm d})italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_T start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ). Since the physical delay time is always smaller than the corresponding auxiliary one, td<T⁢(td)subscript𝑡d𝑇subscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}<T(t_{\rm d})italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_T ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ). i⁢i𝑖𝑖iiitalic_i italic_i) Since the CCBH effects are expected to become larger as the delay-time increases, T⁢(t)𝑇𝑡T(t)italic_T ( italic_t ) needs to increase faster than linearly. i⁢i⁢i𝑖𝑖𝑖iiiitalic_i italic_i italic_i) The minimum and maximum limits of the physical delay-time tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT are assumed to be known from physical considerations (e.g., considering the maximum redshift at which BBHs can be formed, simulations on the minimum physical time that stellar-formed BBHs need to merge, etc…). They are denoted by tminsubscript𝑡mint_{\rm min}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, respectively. i⁢v𝑖𝑣ivitalic_i italic_v) The auxiliary delay time is expected to describe the physics with k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0, thus its distribution is assumed to be log-uniform and given by the PDF

π~⁢(t~d)=1ln⁡(t~max/t~min)⁢1t~d,~𝜋subscript~𝑡d1subscript~𝑡maxsubscript~𝑡min1subscript~𝑡d\tilde{\pi}(\tilde{t}_{\rm d})=\frac{1}{\ln\left({\tilde{t}_{\rm max}}/{\tilde% {t}_{\rm min}}\right)}\frac{1}{\tilde{t}_{\rm d}}\,,over~ start_ARG italic_π end_ARG ( over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = divide start_ARG 1 end_ARG start_ARG roman_ln ( over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) end_ARG divide start_ARG 1 end_ARG start_ARG over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG , (24)

where t~max≡T⁢(tmax)subscript~𝑡max𝑇subscript𝑡max\tilde{t}_{\rm max}\equiv T(t_{\rm max})over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≡ italic_T ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) and t~min≡T⁢(tmin)subscript~𝑡min𝑇subscript𝑡min\tilde{t}_{\rm min}\equiv T(t_{\rm min})over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≡ italic_T ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) are the maximum and minimum values of the log-uniform distribution. Since t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is simply an auxiliary quantity, computed assuming that k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0, t~maxsubscript~𝑡max\tilde{t}_{\rm max}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT can be larger than the age of the universe.

The four considerations above are more general than the specific assumptions of Ghodla et al. (2023) and they are sufficiently precise to yield that log⁡tdsubscript𝑡d\log t_{\rm d}roman_log italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is not flatly distributed, apart from a specific case, and in general the log⁡tdsubscript𝑡d\log t_{\rm d}roman_log italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is crescent. Indeed, since there is a continuous one-to-one map between tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and t~dsubscript~𝑡d\tilde{t}_{\rm d}over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, the probability of finding a physical tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT between the values tminsubscript𝑡mint_{\rm min}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and t𝑡titalic_t can be found from π~~𝜋\tilde{\pi}over~ start_ARG italic_π end_ARG,

P⁢(t)=∫tmintπ⁢(td)⁢dtd=∫t~mint~π~⁢(t~d)⁢dt~d,𝑃𝑡superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑡min𝑡𝜋subscript𝑡ddifferential-dsubscript𝑡dsuperscriptsubscriptsubscript~𝑡min~𝑡~𝜋subscript~𝑡ddifferential-dsubscript~𝑡dP(t)=\int_{t_{\rm min}}^{t}\pi(t_{\rm d})\,{\rm d}t_{\rm d}=\int_{\tilde{t}_{% \rm min}}^{\tilde{t}}\tilde{\pi}(\tilde{t}_{\rm d})\,{\rm d}\tilde{t}_{\rm d},italic_P ( italic_t ) = ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_t end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_π ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) roman_d italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = ∫ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT over~ start_ARG italic_π end_ARG ( over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) roman_d over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , (25)

where π⁢(td)𝜋subscript𝑡d\pi(t_{\rm d})italic_π ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) is the a priori unknown PDF of tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and t~=T⁢(t)~𝑡𝑇𝑡\tilde{t}=T(t)over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG = italic_T ( italic_t ). Therefore, using eq. (24), followed by a redefinition of units,

P⁢(t)=1ln⁡(t~max/t~min)⁢ln⁡(T⁢(t)t~min)=ln⁡T⁢(t)ln⁡t~max.𝑃𝑡1subscript~𝑡maxsubscript~𝑡min𝑇𝑡subscript~𝑡min𝑇𝑡subscript~𝑡maxP(t)=\frac{1}{\ln\left({\tilde{t}_{\rm max}}/{\tilde{t}_{\rm min}}\right)}\ln% \left(\frac{T(t)}{\tilde{t}_{\rm min}}\right)=\frac{\ln T(t)}{\ln{\tilde{t}_{% \rm max}}}\,.italic_P ( italic_t ) = divide start_ARG 1 end_ARG start_ARG roman_ln ( over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT / over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) end_ARG roman_ln ( divide start_ARG italic_T ( italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG ) = divide start_ARG roman_ln italic_T ( italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG roman_ln over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG . (26)

About the units redefinition, one can measure tmaxsubscript𝑡maxt_{\rm max}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and t𝑡titalic_t in units of tminsubscript𝑡mint_{\rm min}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, which is in practice equivalent to tmin=1subscript𝑡min1t_{\rm min}=1italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1. Moreover, since eq. (26) is invariant under T⁢(t)→k⁢T⁢(t)→𝑇𝑡𝑘𝑇𝑡T(t)\to kT(t)italic_T ( italic_t ) → italic_k italic_T ( italic_t ) and t~min=T⁢(1)subscript~𝑡min𝑇1\tilde{t}_{\rm min}=T(1)over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_T ( 1 ), one can set t~min=1subscript~𝑡min1\tilde{t}_{\rm min}=1over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1 in this equation. These units transformations are not necessary, but may be helpful to understand the essence of the argument.

Now we compare the above probability to a log-uniform probability defined within 1=tmin<t<max1subscript𝑡min𝑡subscriptmaxabsent1=t_{\rm min}<t<_{\rm max}1 = italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min end_POSTSUBSCRIPT < italic_t < start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and denoted by P¯⁢(t)¯𝑃𝑡\bar{P}(t)over¯ start_ARG italic_P end_ARG ( italic_t ),

P⁢(t)P¯⁢(t)=ln⁡tmaxln⁡t~max⁢ln⁡T⁢(t)ln⁡t.𝑃𝑡¯𝑃𝑡subscript𝑡maxsubscript~𝑡max𝑇𝑡𝑡\frac{P(t)}{\bar{P}(t)}=\frac{\ln{t_{\rm max}}}{\ln{\tilde{t}_{\rm max}}}\frac% {\ln T(t)}{\ln t}\,.divide start_ARG italic_P ( italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG over¯ start_ARG italic_P end_ARG ( italic_t ) end_ARG = divide start_ARG roman_ln italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG start_ARG roman_ln over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_ARG divide start_ARG roman_ln italic_T ( italic_t ) end_ARG start_ARG roman_ln italic_t end_ARG . (27)

If ln⁡T⁢(t)𝑇𝑡\ln T(t)roman_ln italic_T ( italic_t ) increases faster than ln⁡t𝑡\ln troman_ln italic_t for all t𝑡titalic_t, then P⁢(t)/P¯⁢(t)𝑃𝑡¯𝑃𝑡{P(t)}/{\bar{P}(t)}italic_P ( italic_t ) / over¯ start_ARG italic_P end_ARG ( italic_t ) monotonically increases, implying that max⁡[P⁢(t)/P¯⁢(t)]=P⁢(tmax)/P¯⁢(tmax)=1𝑃𝑡¯𝑃𝑡𝑃subscript𝑡max¯𝑃subscript𝑡max1\max[{P(t)}/{\bar{P}(t)}]={P(t_{\rm max})}/{\bar{P}(t_{\rm max})}=1roman_max [ italic_P ( italic_t ) / over¯ start_ARG italic_P end_ARG ( italic_t ) ] = italic_P ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) / over¯ start_ARG italic_P end_ARG ( italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = 1. That is, P⁢(t)<P¯⁢(t)𝑃𝑡¯𝑃𝑡P(t)<\bar{P}(t)italic_P ( italic_t ) < over¯ start_ARG italic_P end_ARG ( italic_t ) for any t<tmax𝑡subscript𝑡maxt<t_{\rm max}italic_t < italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. This implies that the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution will not be log-uniform and that it will favour larger tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT values with respect to the log-uniform case.

A special T⁢(t)𝑇𝑡T(t)italic_T ( italic_t ) function that is in agreement with ∂t[T⁢(t)/t]>0subscript𝑡delimited-[]𝑇𝑡𝑡0\partial_{t}[T(t)/t]>0∂ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_t end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [ italic_T ( italic_t ) / italic_t ] > 0 (as implied by the item i⁢i𝑖𝑖iiitalic_i italic_i above) but ∂t[ln⁡T⁢(t)/ln⁡t]=0subscript𝑡delimited-[]𝑇𝑡𝑡0\partial_{t}[\ln T(t)/\ln t]=0∂ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_t end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [ roman_ln italic_T ( italic_t ) / roman_ln italic_t ] = 0 is T⁢(t)=tn𝑇𝑡superscript𝑡𝑛T(t)=t^{n}italic_T ( italic_t ) = italic_t start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_n end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, with n>1𝑛1n>1italic_n > 1 and 1<t<tmax1𝑡subscript𝑡max1<t<t_{\rm max}1 < italic_t < italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. For this case, although T⁢(t)𝑇𝑡T(t)italic_T ( italic_t ) increases faster than t𝑡titalic_t, ln⁡T⁢(t)𝑇𝑡\ln T(t)roman_ln italic_T ( italic_t ) increases proportionally to ln⁡t𝑡\ln troman_ln italic_t, implying a constant P/P¯𝑃¯𝑃P/\bar{P}italic_P / over¯ start_ARG italic_P end_ARG ratio. Indeed, computing eq. (27) directly, ln⁡t~max=n⁢ln⁡tmaxsubscript~𝑡max𝑛subscript𝑡max\ln\tilde{t}_{\rm max}=n\ln t_{\rm max}roman_ln over~ start_ARG italic_t end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = italic_n roman_ln italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and ln⁡T⁢(t)=n⁢ln⁡t𝑇𝑡𝑛𝑡\ln T(t)=n\ln troman_ln italic_T ( italic_t ) = italic_n roman_ln italic_t, hence P⁢(t)/P¯⁢(t)=1𝑃𝑡¯𝑃𝑡1{P(t)}/{\bar{P}(t)}=1italic_P ( italic_t ) / over¯ start_ARG italic_P end_ARG ( italic_t ) = 1. A CCBH correction that can be approximated by T⁢(t)=tn𝑇𝑡superscript𝑡𝑛T(t)=t^{n}italic_T ( italic_t ) = italic_t start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_n end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT will not change the log-uniform distribution. Exponentials and polynomials, for example, when used to build monotonically increasing T⁢(t)𝑇𝑡T(t)italic_T ( italic_t ) functions, will change the delay-time distribution and will increase the odds of large delay times with respect to the log-uniform case.

We remark that T⁢(t)𝑇𝑡T(t)italic_T ( italic_t ) functions that satisfy the item i⁢i𝑖𝑖iiitalic_i italic_i (i.e., ∂t[T⁢(t)/t]>0subscript𝑡delimited-[]𝑇𝑡𝑡0\partial_{t}[T(t)/t]>0∂ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_t end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [ italic_T ( italic_t ) / italic_t ] > 0), cannot lead to ∂t[ln⁡T⁢(t)/ln⁡t]<0subscript𝑡delimited-[]𝑇𝑡𝑡0\partial_{t}[\ln T(t)/\ln t]<0∂ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_t end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [ roman_ln italic_T ( italic_t ) / roman_ln italic_t ] < 0.

Provided that the four (i𝑖iitalic_i-i⁢v𝑖𝑣ivitalic_i italic_v) general hypothesis listed above are satisfied, and that a log-uniform distribution is a good approximation for the standard (k=0𝑘0k=0italic_k = 0) case, we conclude that the CCBH correction to the standard delay time should either preserve the log-uniform distribution and our results from the Secs. 4 and 5, or strength the constraints we find.


Appendix C Changing the delay time distribution slope

The delay time distribution is a crucial assumption, so we discuss here briefly the impact of changing the 1/td1subscript𝑡𝑑1/t_{d}1 / italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT slope. This case is different from the one studied in Appendix B, since here we do not consider CCBH corrections to the log-uniform distribution, but a direct change in the tdsubscript𝑡dt_{\rm d}italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT distribution.

The full functional form of the relation delay times versus mass is unknown and it appears difficult to model exactly. Here we limit ourselves to a preliminary investigation. For this appendix we adopt the more conservative direct method of Sec. 4. van Son et al. (2022) show a tendency for low-mass BHs to be formed via the common envelope channel, and this channel would have a delay time distribution steeper than 1/td1subscript𝑡𝑑1/t_{d}1 / italic_t start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_d end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (i.e., shorter delay times on average), corresponding to β=1.1−1.3𝛽1.11.3\beta=1.1-1.3italic_β = 1.1 - 1.3. Looking at the results of App. B of van Son et al. (2022), we see that such a steeper power-law distribution approximates the predicted behavior for masses below 30⁢M⊙30subscript𝑀direct-product30M_{\odot}30 italic_M start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⊙ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. For simplicity, we assume that this power-law extends to all masses: this has anyway very little impact since large masses are not the main drivers of our statistics. In Fig. 16 we show the probability contour plot for w,β𝑤𝛽w,\betaitalic_w , italic_β. Negative β𝛽\betaitalic_β seems totally excluded. For β≥1.2𝛽1.2\beta\geq 1.2italic_β ≥ 1.2, the probability for w=−1𝑤1w=-1italic_w = - 1 decreases below 2σ𝜎\sigmaitalic_σ, bringing the DEBH model into the non-rejection region. Therefore, as far as current data are concerned, such steeper power-laws might alleviate or solve the tension.
